subject: maths
title: Probability

nodes.title: Compute the theoretical probability of an event
nodes.brief: Combine favourable count and total count into P(E) = favourable/total, then reduce the fraction.
nodes.quote: P(E) = Number of outcomes favourable to E / Number of all possible outcomes of the experiment ,
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Identify the event and count total outcomes.
Name the event E precisely, then list or recall every equally-likely outcome of the experiment to get the denominator. For a fair die the total is 6; for a shuffled deck it is 52; for two coins it is 4.

PART 2 — Count favourable outcomes and form the ratio.
Pick every outcome that satisfies E and count them to get the numerator, then write P(E) = favourable/total. Reduce to lowest terms: P(ace) = 4/52 = 1/13, P(number > 4 on a die) = 2/6 = 1/3.

PART 3 — Check the answer lies in [0, 1].
A probability can never be negative or exceed 1. If your fraction is outside [0, 1], recheck the counts before reporting.
keyMoves: Identify the event E whose probability is asked
Count the number of all possible (equally likely) outcomes for the denominator
Count the outcomes favourable to E for the numerator
Write P(E) = favourable/total and reduce
misconceptions: Using number of trials (empirical) instead of equally-likely outcomes (theoretical)
Putting total over favourable (inverting the ratio)
Forgetting to reduce or mis-reducing the fraction

nodes.title: Count the total number of equally likely outcomes
nodes.brief: List every distinct outcome of the experiment; that count becomes the denominator of P(E).
nodes.quote: Now, the number of possible outcomes = 3.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — List all distinct outcomes of the experiment.
For every random experiment, first write down each possible result: for a single die that is {1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6}; for a 3-ball bag (red, blue, green) that is {R, B, G}. The size of this list is the denominator.

PART 2 — Confirm every outcome is equally likely.
The denominator is only valid under the equally-likely assumption. If the bag has 4 red and 1 blue ball, 'red' and 'blue' are not equally likely, so you must count individual balls (5 total), not colours (2 total).

PART 3 — Use the count as the denominator.
Write the total as the bottom of the probability fraction. A 52-card deck gives denominator 52; two coins give denominator 4; a bag of 9 marbles gives denominator 9.
keyMoves: List or count every distinct outcome of the experiment
Confirm all listed outcomes are equally likely
Use that count as the denominator of P(E)
misconceptions: Counting outcomes that are not equally likely (e.g. 'red or blue' from a 4-red 1-blue bag)
Miscounting the deck or bag total
Double-counting or omitting an outcome

nodes.title: Count the outcomes favourable to an event
nodes.brief: From the sample space, pick only those outcomes that make the event happen and count them.
nodes.quote: The number of outcomes favourable to E = 4
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Read the event description precisely.
Understand exactly what the event requires: 'greater than 4' means 5 and 6 only (not 4 itself); 'an ace' means any of the 4 aces; 'a red card' means any of the 26 hearts or diamonds.

PART 2 — Select matching outcomes from the sample space.
Go through the full list of outcomes and tick every one that satisfies the event. For 'number > 4' on a die, tick 5 and 6 — that gives 2 favourable outcomes. For aces in a 52-card deck, tick all 4 aces.

PART 3 — Count the ticked outcomes to get the numerator.
The total of your ticks is the numerator of P(E). P(ace) = 4/52 = 1/13; P(number > 4) = 2/6 = 1/3.
keyMoves: Read the event description precisely
Pick out every outcome that satisfies the event
Count them to get the numerator
misconceptions: Including outcomes that do not satisfy the event
Missing boundary cases (e.g. 'greater than 4' includes 5 and 6 only, not 4)
Confusing favourable with total

nodes.title: Form and reduce the probability fraction
nodes.brief: Place favourable over total and divide both by their HCF to reach lowest terms.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Write the raw fraction favourable/total.
Once both counts are in hand, write them as a fraction: 2 favourable out of 6 gives 2/6, and 4 aces out of 52 gives 4/52. This raw fraction is a valid probability but usually not fully simplified.

PART 2 — Find the HCF and cancel.
Divide numerator and denominator by their highest common factor: HCF(2, 6) = 2, so 2/6 = 1/3; HCF(4, 52) = 4, so 4/52 = 1/13. Always check that the reduced fraction still lies in [0, 1].

PART 3 — Report the simplified fraction as the final answer.
Write P(E) in lowest terms. If the question accepts a decimal, convert: 1/3 ≈ 0.333, 1/13 ≈ 0.077. Never report a value outside [0, 1].
keyMoves: Place favourable count over total count
Divide numerator and denominator by their common factor
Express the reduced fraction (or decimal)
misconceptions: Leaving the fraction unreduced when a reduced form is expected
Cancelling incorrectly
Reporting a value outside [0,1]

nodes.title: Decide whether outcomes are equally likely
nodes.brief: Check for symmetry or fairness; only then is each outcome equally likely and the classical formula valid.
nodes.quote: So, the outcomes (a red ball or a blue ball) are not equally likely.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Check the mechanism for symmetry or fairness.
A fair coin gives H and T the same chance because it is symmetric; a balanced die gives each face the same chance. Ask: is there any physical or logical reason one outcome would occur more often than another?

PART 2 — Identify cases where equal likelihood fails.
If a bag has 4 red balls and 1 blue ball, drawing 'red' is four times as likely as drawing 'blue' — they are not equally likely outcomes. Similarly, 'a car starts' and 'a car does not start' are not automatically 50-50.

PART 3 — Only apply the classical formula when equally likely holds.
P(E) = favourable/total is valid only when every listed outcome has the same probability. If equal likelihood fails, you need relative frequencies or additional information to proceed.
keyMoves: Check for symmetry / fairness of the mechanism
Reject the equally-likely assumption when outcomes differ in chance
Apply the classical formula only when equally likely holds
misconceptions: Assuming any two-way outcome (starts/doesn't, makes/misses) is 50-50
Treating 'red or blue' as equally likely when counts differ

nodes.title: Apply the complement rule P(not E) = 1 - P(E)
nodes.brief: Subtract P(E) from 1 to find the probability of the opposite event without re-counting.
nodes.quote: P(E) + P(E̅ ) = 1, which gives us P(E̅ ) = 1 – P(E).
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Recognise E and 'not E' as complementary.
Two events are complementary when together they cover every possible outcome and they do not overlap. 'Getting an ace' and 'not getting an ace' from a deck are complementary; so are 'Reshma wins' and 'Reshma does not win' in a two-player race.

PART 2 — Compute P(not E) by subtracting from 1.
Since P(E) + P(not E) = 1, you get P(not E) = 1 - P(E). If P(ace) = 1/13 then P(not ace) = 1 - 1/13 = 12/13. If P(Reshma wins) = 0.62 then P(Reshma does not win) = 1 - 0.62 = 0.38.

PART 3 — Use this rule when the complement is easier to count.
The rule saves work: instead of counting all non-ace cards (48), just subtract 1/13 from 1. Whenever 'not E' is harder to count directly, flip to the complement.
keyMoves: Recognise E and 'not E' as complementary
Subtract the known probability from 1
Report 1 - P(E) as P(not E)
misconceptions: Using events that are not actually complementary
Subtracting the count instead of the probability
Forgetting that the two must cover all outcomes

nodes.title: Recognise an impossible event has probability 0
nodes.brief: When no outcome in the sample space satisfies the event, the favourable count is 0 so P(E) = 0.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Check whether any outcome satisfies the event.
Ask: is there even one outcome in the sample space that makes the event happen? Rolling a 7 on a standard die has no matching outcome in {1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6}. Getting a sum of 13 on two dice has no matching pair in the 36-outcome grid.

PART 2 — Set the numerator to 0 when no outcome qualifies.
If the answer to the check is 'none', write favourable = 0. Then P(E) = 0/total = 0 regardless of how large the total is. The event is called an impossible event.

PART 3 — Distinguish impossible from merely unlikely.
An impossible event literally cannot occur under the experiment (P = 0). A very unlikely event — say, rolling six sixes in a row — has a tiny but positive probability. Never assign P = 0 to an event just because it seems hard.
keyMoves: Check whether any outcome satisfies the event
If none, set numerator to 0
Conclude P(E) = 0 (impossible event)
misconceptions: Thinking a rare event has probability 0
Assuming every described event must have a positive probability

nodes.title: Recognise a sure event has probability 1
nodes.brief: When every outcome in the sample space satisfies the event, P(E) = total/total = 1.
nodes.quote: P(E) = P(getting a number less than 7) = 6/6 = 1
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Check whether every outcome satisfies the event.
Ask: does every element of the sample space make the event happen? Getting a number less than 7 on a die matches all of {1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6}. Getting a sum of 12 or less on two dice matches all 36 pairs.

PART 2 — Set numerator equal to the total when every outcome qualifies.
If every outcome matches, favourable = total, so P(E) = total/total = 1. This event is called a sure or certain event — it is guaranteed to happen.

PART 3 — Distinguish certain from very likely.
A sure event has P = 1 because every outcome in the sample space satisfies it. An event with P = 0.99 is very likely but not certain — there is still one outcome that fails it.
keyMoves: Check whether all outcomes satisfy the event
If so, set numerator equal to denominator
Conclude P(E) = 1 (sure event)
misconceptions: Confusing 'very likely' with 'certain'
Failing to see that every outcome qualifies

nodes.title: Apply the bound 0 ≤ P(E) ≤ 1
nodes.brief: Every probability must lie between 0 and 1 inclusive; any value outside this range is invalid.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Understand why probabilities are bounded.
The favourable count is at least 0 (impossible event) and at most the total (sure event). Dividing by the total gives a fraction between 0/total = 0 and total/total = 1. No other values are possible for a theoretical probability.

PART 2 — Reject values outside [0, 1].
If a calculation yields -0.3 or 1.7, a mistake was made — recheck the counts. Values like -1.5 or 5/3 cannot represent a probability. The boundary values 0 and 1 are themselves valid (impossible and sure events).

PART 3 — Recognise valid probabilities in different forms.
Fractions, decimals, and percentages can all be valid: 3/4, 0.75, and 75% all represent the same probability. Check that the value lies in [0, 1] (or equivalently [0%, 100%]) before accepting it.
keyMoves: Compare a candidate value against the interval [0,1]
Reject negatives and values exceeding 1
Accept fractions, percentages and decimals within range
misconceptions: Accepting a negative or >1 value as a probability
Not recognising 15% or 0.7 as valid

nodes.title: Use that elementary-event probabilities sum to 1
nodes.brief: The probabilities of all single-outcome (elementary) events of an experiment must add to exactly 1.
nodes.quote: Observe that the sum of the probabilities of all the elementary events of an experiment is 1.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Identify the elementary events of the experiment.
An elementary event is an event containing exactly one outcome. For a 3-ball bag with one white (W), one black (B), and one red (R) ball, the elementary events are {W}, {B}, {R}, with probabilities 1/3 each.

PART 2 — Verify (or use) the fact that they sum to 1.
Since every outcome belongs to exactly one elementary event and all outcomes are covered, P(W) + P(B) + P(R) = 1/3 + 1/3 + 1/3 = 1. Use this as a check: if the sum is not 1, at least one probability is wrong.

PART 3 — Find a missing probability using the sum.
If two of three elementary event probabilities are given (say P(W) = 1/3, P(B) = 1/2) the missing one is 1 - 1/3 - 1/2 = 1/6. This is the main application of the rule at Class 10 level.
keyMoves: Identify the elementary events of the experiment
Add their probabilities
Verify the sum is 1 (or solve for a missing one)
misconceptions: Thinking compound events also must sum to 1 across an arbitrary list
Assuming each of 11 dice-sums has probability 1/11

nodes.title: List the sample space for two coins tossed together
nodes.brief: Treat the two coins as distinct and list all four ordered pairs: (H,H),(H,T),(T,H),(T,T).
nodes.quote: the possible outcomes are (H, H), (H, T), (T, H), (T, T), which are all equally likely.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Treat the two coins as distinguishable.
Label the coins 'first' and 'second'. The first coin can show H or T, and independently the second coin can show H or T, giving 2 × 2 = 4 outcomes. Treating the coins as identical collapses (H,T) and (T,H) into one outcome, which is wrong.

PART 2 — List all four ordered pairs.
The complete sample space is {(H,H), (H,T), (T,H), (T,T)}, all equally likely with probability 1/4 each. 'At least one head' covers (H,H), (H,T), (T,H) — three outcomes, so P = 3/4.

PART 3 — Count favourable pairs for the event.
For any event, tick the pairs that satisfy it and count: 'exactly two heads' → only (H,H), P = 1/4; 'at least one tail' → (H,T),(T,H),(T,T), P = 3/4.
keyMoves: Treat the two coins as distinguishable (first, second)
List all four ordered pairs of H/T
Count favourable pairs for the event
misconceptions: Collapsing (H,T) and (T,H) into one outcome and getting 3 outcomes
Assigning probability 1/3 to two-heads/two-tails/one-of-each

nodes.title: List the sample space for two dice thrown together
nodes.brief: Build the 6×6 grid of 36 ordered pairs (die1, die2) and use it to count event outcomes.
nodes.quote: So, the number of possible outcomes = 6 × 6 = 36.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Treat the two dice as distinguishable.
Label them die 1 (blue) and die 2 (grey). Each shows a face 1-6, giving 6 × 6 = 36 ordered pairs. The pair (1, 4) differs from (4, 1) because they come from different dice.

PART 2 — Build the 6×6 grid of outcomes.
Row = die 1 face (1-6), column = die 2 face (1-6). Every cell is one equally-likely outcome. Sum = 8 is achieved by (2,6),(3,5),(4,4),(5,3),(6,2) — that is 5 cells, so P(sum=8) = 5/36.

PART 3 — Count the cells matching the target event.
Locate and count every cell that satisfies the event. A common mistake is treating the 11 possible sums (2 through 12) as equally likely — they are not; P(sum=7) = 6/36 but P(sum=2) = 1/36.
keyMoves: Treat the dice as distinguishable
Form all 6 × 6 = 36 ordered pairs
Count pairs satisfying the target event (e.g. sum 8)
misconceptions: Counting unordered pairs and getting fewer than 36
Treating the 11 possible sums as equally likely

nodes.title: Use the composition of a 52-card deck
nodes.brief: Know: 52 cards total, 4 suits of 13, 4 aces, 12 face cards (K/Q/J), 26 red and 26 black.
nodes.quote: It consists of 52 cards which are divided into 4 suits of 13 cards each — spades (♠), hearts (♥), diamonds (♦) and clubs (♣).
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Know the overall structure: 52 cards in 4 suits.
A standard deck has 52 cards divided equally into four suits: spades (black), clubs (black), hearts (red), diamonds (red). Each suit contains 13 cards: Ace, 2-10, Jack, Queen, King.

PART 2 — Know the key counts.
4 Aces (one per suit); 12 face cards (Jack, Queen, King in each of the 4 suits = 3 × 4 = 12); 26 red cards (hearts + diamonds); 26 black cards (spades + clubs). Aces are NOT face cards.

PART 3 — Use counts as numerators or denominators.
Total = 52 (always the denominator for one-card draws). P(ace) = 4/52 = 1/13; P(face card) = 12/52 = 3/13; P(queen of hearts) = 1/52; P(red card) = 26/52 = 1/2.
keyMoves: Recall 52 total, 4 suits of 13
Identify counts: 4 aces, 12 face cards, 26 red, etc.
Feed the relevant count into the probability ratio
misconceptions: Counting aces as face cards
Mis-stating the number of face cards or red cards

nodes.title: Compute probability as a ratio of lengths
nodes.brief: For a continuous outcome on a line segment, P(E) = (length of favourable interval) / (total length).
nodes.quote: So, P(E) = Distance favourable to the event E / Total distance in which outcomes can lie = (1/2)/2 = 1/4
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Identify the total interval of possible outcomes.
When the outcome is a real number drawn uniformly from an interval (e.g. a bus arrives at any time in [0, 2] minutes), the sample space is that interval and its length is the denominator.

PART 2 — Identify the favourable sub-interval.
Determine which part of the total interval satisfies the event. If the event is 'arrives in the first half-minute', the favourable interval is [0, 0.5] with length 0.5.

PART 3 — Divide favourable length by total length.
P(E) = 0.5 / 2 = 1/4. The counting formula P = favourable/total is replaced by a length ratio because there are infinitely many point outcomes — you cannot count them individually.
keyMoves: Identify the total interval of possible outcomes
Identify the favourable sub-interval
Divide favourable length by total length
misconceptions: Trying to count infinitely many point outcomes
Using the wrong interval endpoints

nodes.title: Compute probability as a ratio of areas
nodes.brief: For a 2-D continuous outcome, P(E) = (favourable area) / (total area).
nodes.quote: Therefore, P (helicopter crashed in the lake) = 7.5/40.5 = 75/405 = 5/27
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Compute the total region area.
The total area is the entire region where the outcome can occur. In the helicopter example, the total region is a rectangle 4.5 km × 9 km = 40.5 km². This is the denominator.

PART 2 — Compute the favourable region area.
The favourable area is the part of the total region that satisfies the event. The lake has area 7.5 km². This is the numerator.

PART 3 — Divide and reduce.
P(crash in lake) = 7.5/40.5 = 75/405 = 5/27. The same P = favourable/total structure applies, just with areas instead of counts or lengths. Watch out for perimeter vs area errors.
keyMoves: Compute the total region area
Compute the favourable region area
Divide favourable area by total area and reduce
misconceptions: Using perimeter instead of area
Arithmetic slips in area computation

nodes.title: Solve a word problem using the complement
nodes.brief: When the direct event is hard to count, find the easier complementary event and subtract from 1.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Identify which event is easier to compute directly.
In the birthday problem, P(two people share a birthday) is hard to count directly. Its complement — P(two people have different birthdays) — is easy: the second person has 364 out of 365 days that differ from the first, so P(different) = 364/365.

PART 2 — Compute the probability of the easier event.
Calculate the complement's probability using the standard method. Here P(different birthdays) = 364/365.

PART 3 — Subtract from 1 to get the target probability.
P(same birthday) = 1 - 364/365 = 1/365. Always check that the two events truly are complementary — they must together cover all outcomes without overlap.
keyMoves: Identify the easier event to count directly
Compute its probability
Subtract from 1 to get the complementary event
misconceptions: Counting the hard event directly and erring
Forgetting the two events are complementary

narrative:
## Audit performed

I re-read jemh114.txt in full (SEG 0 = lines 4-196, the 14.1 definition plus Examples 1-8 and every rule statement: classical-probability definition, complement, impossible/sure events, the 0≤P(E)≤1 bound, the elementary-event sum, and the 52-card-deck description; SEG 1 = lines 198-280, Examples 9-13 with two coins, geometric length/area, shirts, and the two-dice 36-outcome grid; SEG 2 = Exercise 14.1; SEG 3 = Summary) and the shared maths exam file (Standard SQP+MS, Basic SQP+MS).

## Citation re-verification (the #1 failure mode)

I located every node quote AND every gate quote verbatim in its tagged segment:
- All 16 NODE quotes confirmed character-for-character: e.g. compute-goal → line 36; count-total → "Now, the number of possible outcomes = 3." line 65; count-favourable → line 145; form-ratio → line 84; identify-equally-likely → line 22; complement rule (with the garbled glyph E̅) → line 108; impossible → line 122; sure → line 129; range-bound line 135; elementary-sum line 78; two-coins line 201 (SEG 1); two-dice line 270 (SEG 1); card-deck line 137; geometric-length line 227; geometric-area line 242; complement-word-problem line 173.
- All 16 GATE quotes confirmed. The three exam gates were checked against the Marking Scheme lines literally: Standard Q14 "14. B) 3\n8" = exam lines 346-347; Standard Q18 "18. D) 3\n26" = lines 352-353; Basic Q15 "15. D) 4 1" = line 1087. Chapter gates ("Note that P(W) + P(B) + P(R) = 1." line 196; "Therefore, P(E) = 3/4" line 205; "Hence, P(E) = 5/36" line 274; "= 1/365" line 174; etc.) all verified.

No quote needed re-tagging or replacement — every cited string is a contiguous substring of the segment named.

## Grain / coverage

17 nodes, each a single failable skill. Re-scanning the worked examples I confirmed no lumped node and no missed skill that warrants its own gate: Example 12's "acceptable to Sujatha = 88+8" is just count-favourable over a compound category (covered), and the 'not an ace = 1-1/13' step is apply-complement-rule (covered). card-deck-composition is kept as a bedrock retrieval skill (deck structure) because it genuinely feeds numerator/denominator in Example 4 and exam Q18, not as bare exposition. No history/taxonomy node is present (Laplace/Cardan/Bernoulli paragraph correctly excluded).

## Edges

Single root compute-theoretical-probability (never a child). Graph is acyclic (all parent→child arrows flow toward leaves), every one of the 17 node slugs appears in at least one edge (no orphans), and every edge endpoint is a defined node slug. geometric-area→geometric-length and complement-word-problem→apply-complement-rule are true prerequisites.

## Gates

One gate per node (17/17, 100% coverage). Each grader/expected/answer is self-consistent for the deterministic tester: numeric gates carry the example's decimal value (5/36→0.1389, 5/27→0.1852, 1/365→0.00274) with an appropriate tol, mcq gates carry the marking-scheme option as `correct` and within `options` (3/8, 4, 3/26, plus the chapter-derived equally-likely yes/no). Arithmetic re-checked: white ball 9/24=3/8, red face card 6/52=3/26.

## Result

The draft passed audit unchanged on citations, edges, grain and gate consistency. I corrected only a trivial wording in the elementary-sum gate prompt (comma) for readability; all load-bearing fields are retained.
absentNotes: The chapter does NOT teach (so these are omitted, not failed): the addition rule P(A or B) for mutually exclusive events; conditional probability or 'given that'; independence/multiplication rule; drawing without replacement as a taught method (it appears only as Exercise 15(ii) and 17(ii) problems, with no worked solution stating the method); tree diagrams; odds; and empirical/experimental probability computation (mentioned only to contrast with theoretical, via the Class IX recap and the closing 'note to the reader'). The Cardan/Bernoulli/de Moivre/Laplace history paragraph is pure exposition and is intentionally not a node. The full two-dice sum-distribution table (Exercise 22) is an application of list-sample-space-two-dice, not a separate taught skill. Spinner, piggy-bank, fish-tank and disc-number exercises are all instances of count-favourable / count-total over equally-likely outcomes and add no new failable skill beyond the captured nodes. Standard SQP Q22 (product-odd with non-standard dice faces 4,6,7,9,11,12) was available as a gate but its method is already covered by count-favourable-outcomes + list-sample-space-two-dice, so the cleaner chapter examples were used for those nodes' gates.
